title: The Human Cell Atlas - Aviv Regev
text:
Aviv Regev:
Yes. So thank you for the opportunity to speak
today, and to try and pitch to you the human
cell atlas. So why cells? Well, cells are
our core unit of each and every one of us,
actually of any living organism on earth.
And our cells come in many different flavors
that we like to classify into different types
by different types of characteristics; their
function, their shape, more recently their
molecular profile. So based on this for example
might distinguish in the immune system, dendritic
cells that are our first line of defense,
from T cells and adaptive immunity, we can
distinguish neurons from glial cells like
astrocytes and so on.
Now, cells are also a key intermediate in
between the genotype and the phenotypes that
we care about so dearly. So for example, a
genetic variant manifesting in immune dendritic
cells could increase the risk on Crohn's disease.
Another genetic variant manifesting in skeletal
muscle cells could end up in muscular dystrophy
and so on. So it becomes completely obvious
that if we actually want to functionally characterize
variants and act upon them we have to know
which cells we are dealing with.
So what would be incredibly handy is if we
had some sort of catalog or atlas of all cells.
But what exactly would be in this kind of
atlas? Well, obviously the first thing that
should be there is the listing of all of the
different cell types so we want to have our
neurons and out different immune cells, and
so on. We also want to be able to distinguish
between different states for the same cell
type. For example, an immune cell that is
completely naïve compared to the same immune
cell type after it had encountered a bacterium.
We want to be able to position all of these
cells in their three dimensional locations.
We want to be able to identify the salient
features as these cells undergo dynamic transitions,
for example during differentiation or when
they’re activated. And finally in an ideal
world we'll also be able to trace their entire
lineage as people can easily do for the worm
C. elegans.
I think it's very fair to say that each and
every one of these five aspects our knowledge
about it is limited, some less, some more.
It's also very rarely informed by genomic
information. It’s mostly done by pre-genomic
tools. Some of it is, you know, years old,
a lot of it is decades old, and some of it
is actually, you know, centuries old.
But very recently, advances in single cell
genomics all of a sudden make a much more
systematic catalog within reach. And this
is because, first of all, we have new technologies,
molecular biology and the volume of sequencing
that is needed in order to measure the transcriptions
of single cells with great precision. We also
have sample preparation methods including
the ability to isolate cells on multi-well
plates and handle them with liquid handlers
and microfluidics that let us process a lot
of cells in the right scale and for the right
cost.
And finally, we have rapidly maturing computational
methods for everything from QC to cell type
classification to identifying markers. And
as a result even though single cell genomics
is very young, it’s already taking on very
quickly across the broadest scientific community.
And just based on numbers from the Broad [spelled
phonetically] where I'm at, if at May 2012
we had 18 cells profiled, then by now we have
profiled about 100,000 cells.
Now very importantly we're also making very
quick advances in critical additional capabilities
that will bring us the right scale in resolution.
So for example -- hopefully this works, yes
-- so for example, using reverse homologen
droplets, which is what we see in this movie,
we can handle cells very quickly, at the rate
of thousands per second and with clever bar
coding we ca bring down the cost of sample
prep below three cents a cell.
In addition advances from many different groups
are showing that we can measure many other
variables besides RNA in single cells. And
finally, other advances are able to use experimental
methods to likely register cells to 2D or
3D position.
Now, these advances are already quickly leading
to new insights. So here for example we are
seeing profiles from 800 -- roughly 800 dendritic
cells isolated from the blood of an Asian-American
24 year-old female. The vast majority of these
cells allow us to identify completely from
scratch all the foreign known subtypes of
dendritic cells in the blood. But there is
also a small minority population, under 2
percent or so, that is quite distinct and
can be identified with a unique signature.
These cells are only 2 percent of dendritic
cells they're only 0.06 percent of peripheral
blood mono nuclear cells. But they are validated
readily in 10 of 10 independent individuals.
And looking at these profiles we can actually
infer their position in the lineage, and identify
them as a likely new blood precursor of human
dendritic cells.
Now this is one example of an insight around
lineage. We can also identify new neuronal
subtypes. We can find new states for well-known
types like pathogenic T cells, in models of
autoimmunity cell nucleus blastoma with different
states. We can monitor cells during dynamic
transitions in very quick immune responses,
or in longer differentiation processes, in
diposite [spelled phonetically] cells. And
we can even by coupling single cell RNA-Seq
of this aggregated cells from basically a
piece of tissue together with clever informatics--
again, let me try to run these for you, we
can actually map these cells back to their
three-dimensional positions which is actually
what we see in these rotating spheres that
represent the zebra fish embryo with about
the 10,000 cell stage.
So this all suggests that the sequencer may
very well become the microscope for the 21st
century. And what I want to do in my other
five minutes is to try and suggest how we
could put this microscope for very good use
in order to build ourselves a human cell atlas
that gives us all of the types, states, transitions,
and locations of cells.
But first, let me remind you why we would
actually want such and atlas. And this is
because this kind of atlas will be a reference
map for all functional studies. If will provide
us foundational fundamental knowledge across
all biological systems. It will allow us to
characterize the function of genetic variance,
which is what this community cares about deeply
in the relevant cell type. It will allow us
to interpret pathology and heterogeneity in
disease with respect to an actual reference
of normal. And it should eventually allow
us to characterize this kind of heterogeneity
in individual patients.
I also want to claim that this can only be
done within the context of one of those large
scale manage consortium type technology advancing
projects -- all of these underlying terms
are actually from Eric Green's presentation
earlier today, that the NHGRI excels at, and
really shows the way at, compared to other
institutes.
So first of all let me say a few words about
how. It’s a unified project I probably don't
have to sell you so much on the idea that
it's large. And because it is so large I suggest
that we should probably first engage in a
pilot project in a few complimentary systems.
Maybe the blood, which is easily accessible
but heterogeneous. Maybe the gut which has
a lot of different type of cells, immunity,
epithelial, neural, others, all embedded in
a three-dimensional tissue. Maybe the liver.
Obviously we need to do it in partnership
with the expert communities and the relevant
institutes. We want to do it with a standardized
controlled process. And by developing and
deploying shared analytical tools. And finally
we could realistically set ourselves the goal
of driving the cost per cell to about 15 cents.
And this is already quite realistic with the
ability of using these droplets and with the
yield of a high Seq 10X. And if sequencing
cost drops even further then this number can
go substantially lower.
Why do we want to do it as a big large consortium-managed
project? Well, because in this case there
are fundamental scientific and impact related
reasons that for which only a large scale
project makes sense. So first of all from
a scientific perspective only if we do it
in a standardized way from sampling acquisition,
to sample prep, to QC, to analytics would
we ever have a chance of actually creating
a meaningful catalog that is not riddled by
noise. And our body didn’t hear that it
actually belongs to different institutes.
So almost in any system that you would isolate,
you would find cells that belong to more than
one.
Now, scale serves a lot of purposes. One of
them is obviously to drive the cost down and
that's very important, but only under the
umbrella of scale do you usually actually
garner the energy and the focus needed in
order to have those technology advancing things.
In our case novel approaches to sample prep
and to cell isolation and analytical tools
and there are the things that eventually will
be served to our community as resources to
the entire community of biomedical researchers
and should be commensurate with the clinic.
And then finally, because obviously this is
a large project, I want to spend half a minute
on numbers. So you might not know, but the
human adult consists of about 20 trillion
cells when you don’t count the third of
us that is actually red blood cells that fortunately
don’t have a nucleus so we don't have to
worry about them.
That's a big number. With 50 million dollars,
that's what I gave myself instead of 100 or
at least 50 million that we can spend directly
on the cell that gives us maybe 500 million
cells at 10 cents a cell. That's only 10 to
the minus fifth of the -- 10 to the minus
five of a human. That sounds a little low
balling.
But there are some encouraging things, like
the fact that we know there are about 300
major cell types. But then again, if you consider
something like the retina, which is one of
the best characterized systems for cell types,
it might consist of three of the 300, but
it actually has 100 sub-subtypes inside them.
So it's defiantly a big project. However,
it's also a finite project. And what is very
critical is the fact that in order to build
an atlas, we don't have to measure every single
cell. In the same sense that when we chart
the map we actually don’t do it in a one-to-one
scale, we do in it a one-to-one hundred, or
one-to-one thousand, the level of resolution
that is needed. And with simple calculations
that we can show we can sample orders of magnitude
less cells and get the right representation
for an appropriate survey.
So in the example of the retina where there
are 150 million neurons in the retina, given
its complexity which is probably unsurpassed
in biology, only 40 thousand would be required
for a sufficient survey.
So I pose it to you that 10 to the minus five
of the human body would not be so bad if done
judiciously. And could give us a very good
resolution of an atlas that the need is enormous,
that the tools are in place, and the goal
is within reach, so we should do it. Thank
you very much.
[applause]
Male Speaker:
Any questions?
Aviv Regev:
Ewan.
Ewan Birney:
Very cool. So in that last bit where you were
looking at the kind of the numbers, you didn't
put the state expansion, the combinatorics
[spelled phonetically] with state, and I mean,
my sense is that these spaces are sort of
complex and fractal and I mean, in other words
they're sort of bound-able, but if one wanted
to get more resolution one could always get
more resolution and the question is at what
point are these things useful? And they're
useful I think very early on, I mean, before
one even gets to the scales that you're suggesting.
So my one comment there is about state, the
second comment is combining this with microscopy,
so this is a kind of a new microscope, I buy
that, but I know that there are many pushes
and drive into imaging and microscopy at a
variety of different levels, and especially
at the-- I mean, you have this on the top
left there as well, but I think integrating
this kind of process, especially the model
organisms which are much more tractable, with
high end organismal microscopy would be incredibly
powerful.
Aviv Regev:
I will answer both. The first is about state.
Yeah, state can be a tough one, but state
probably will be saved by the modularity of
the systems that we study, so, yes you could
envision an endless number of stimuli that
would lead to an endless number of variations,
what I think you refer to as a fractal, but
our experience in those places where people
have tried to jiggle biological systems with
these types of continuous responses is yes
that there were variations, but things were
not endlessly variable in terms of the response.
That’s maybe because of canonization, maybe
because of functionality. In transitions this
could be a lot more difficult but I would
say that it would probably beyond the scope
of the first phase of a project like this.
I think in states we could go after some,
we couldn’t go after everything because
we probably don't know all of the necessary
stimuli.
In terms of the relation to microscopy I think
it actually goes very nicely hand-in-hand.
I think that there is a number of technologies,
this one is based on computational mapping
using the crudest in C2 data out there. Straight
images out of paper is on the fish. There
are finer methods that can rely on finer images,
and there are also emerging technologies in
the single cell proteomics that actually couples
slices straight to readout and I think would
be adapted for the RNA domain as well.
Ewan Birney:
Sorry, just to add to that I've been really
impressed by these light sheet approaches,
I don’t know if you’ve seen these light
sheet approaches but the resolution they can
sample at is just incredible --
Aviv Regev:
Yeah. There are phenomenal opportunities there.
Given the 10 minutes I just put one, cute
figure. Ross.
Male Speaker:
Now that I have the chance to study the slide
a little more I see you've already thought
of this very obvious thing, but I want to
give you the opportunity to expand on it,
this should be done in-- I love the idea by
the way, I would do mouse and parallel. I
mean, not wait, but go right ahead. I think
there's a lot of richness that could come
from looking at mouse and then helping us
interpret, you know, to study some mouse and
how to extrapolate that to human.
Aviv Regev:
I think that's a phenomenal suggestion to
do it hand-in-hand with the probably the most
prominent model organism of mammalian biology.
I think it is completely doable, it would
probably also resolve a lot of arguments that
I don't have a strongly held opinion one way
or another but that it would at the very least
tell us where we can go to the mouse as out
model and where we cannot. Thank you. Yes.
Male Speaker:
I just have a couple of pedantic questions
--
Aviv Regev;
Go.
Male Speaker:
One is I noticed on your slide on your cost
estimates you suggested something --
Aviv Regev:
It's somewhere --
Male Speaker:
like 10 to the minus fifth [unintelligible]
or something per cell.
Aviv Regev:
Yeah.
Male Speaker:
And just wanted to get a feeling from you
about how thorough you think that would be.
And second of all, when you think about the
microfluidics solution that you had for lots
of cells you mentioned you've done upwards
of 100 thousand cells, is that system ready
for prime time yet?
Aviv Regev:
Okay, let me answer both questions. The first
was about the cost in the 100 case cell reads
per cell. I think the number of reads per
cell actually depends not just on type of
resolution that you want to have but on the
particular system. And we have pretty good
ideas from the biological literature when
you have to go deep and when you do not. There
is a nice paper earlier in Science this year
Jetten et al. that shows for broad immune
classifications you can very well do with
a thousand reads per cell.
I went higher end because if you talk to a
neuroscientist about distinction for example,
in the retina, which are incredibly important
and functionally distinct entities these neural
sub-subtypes, actually the defining characteristics
are at a much lower level of expression than
the things that typically classify immune
cells, one relative to the other. The current
molecular biology of single cell RNA-Seq the
conversion rates that actually don’t really
vary from protocol to protocol really say
that above 500 thousand cells all you are
really doing is sequencing duplicate reads,
so that doesn’t help, and it seems to us
from our own analysis I don't speak for my
own lab only but for the community of people
who works with single cell RNA-Seq data that
you get the best bang for your buck from the
first hundred thousand and then its diminishing
returns.
So that's our current knowledge. If our sequencing
were a lot better we should go with more reads,
but were just not there. If our sequencing
for molecular biology was better. About the
drops. No, the 100,000 cells were done by
a mixture. Some of them with microfluidics,
some of them with regular plates followed
by automated robot, you know, liquid handling,
and some of them were first done, you know,
by hand, but nobody does them by hand anymore.
The revers immersion droplets I would not
put on the slide if we didn’t have really
encouraging preliminary results. We think
we turned the corner on them, but we haven’t
used them for the 100,000 count.
Ewan Birney:
One more question.
Aviv Regev:
Yes.
Ewan Birney:
Is there a strategy to advance the lineage
map ahead of the other assessments, or is
it the concept that you have to know the states
and the locations before you can do the lineage
map?
Aviv Regev:
No, actually you don’t have to know the
states nor the locations to do the lineage-ing
according to the people who do lineage-ing.
There are several strategies, most of them
are based on judicious genome sequencing,
not whole genome sequencing, and there doesn't
seem to be an impediment to combining that
with RNA readout of the state of the cell,
which mean that you should be able to infer
lineage. Having said that, it would require
work, I would say, that if I had to say off
the top of my head I would say that the detective
for that and very early proof of concept would
be appropriate for phase one, but not the
full application.
Male Speaker:
Thanks very much Aviv, we have to move on.
[end of transcript]
